w:r.w:t: A design manual for building pathways and sequence abilities with a consistent power budget
for both human authors and AI-assisted drafting
Built for a dnd5e-derived Foundry implementation adapted to Lord of the Mysteries-style progression
Use it as your private framework when writing pathways by hand, and as a standing rules document when asking AI to draft sequence packages.

This guide gives you a repeatable way to build LoTM pathways without forcing every pathway to play the same way. The core principle is simple: each sequence has a power budget, but different pathways spend that budget differently. A Seer can be balanced against a Warrior without having the same damage profile, because the Seer may spend more of its budget on information, setup, subtle control, and narrative leverage.
Use the budget as design currency, not as raw damage. A sequence is balanced when its total influence on the campaign matches its tier.
Do not equate power with damage alone.
Price abilities by practical impact in play.
Every sequence should add a clear fantasy and a clear upgrade delta.
Upper sequences should unlock new kinds of influence, not only larger numbers.
Abstract pathways are valid as long as their leverage is bounded and priced.
The full power allowance for a character at that sequence.
The increase in budget when advancing from the previous sequence. Use this when designing the new sequence package.
How a pathway spends its budget across offense, control, utility, survivability, information, ritual leverage, and narrative authority.
An ability that changes what facts, access, information, or causal leverage a character can obtain.
A cost reduction because the effect is risky, costly, preparation-heavy, or unreliable.

Treat Sequence 9 through Sequence 0 as a cumulative progression. The total budget reflects full expected capability at that sequence. The gain budget reflects how much additional power a promotion should add.
These numbers are tunable. What matters is the shape: modest early growth, stronger mid-tier jumps, and steep late-game increases so Sequence 2, Sequence 1, and Sequence 0 feel qualitatively larger.
Recommended role of each tier band
A few supernatural edges, narrow advantages, limited reliable impact.
Reliable tools, stable encounter influence, signature identity online.
Regional threat or specialist dominance; larger rituals and stronger leverage.
Mythic influence, conceptual edges, campaign-warping impact when prepared.
Qualitative change in authority and scale.
Build each sequence with the same seven-step workflow.
Write a one-sentence sequence fantasy. What does this sequence let the character do that the previous one could not?
Set the target budget. Use the total budget for a full package and the gain budget for a promotion package.
Choose a power vector. Decide how much of the budget goes into conflict, information, movement, ritual leverage, survivability, control, and narrative authority.
Draft 3-6 features: usually one signature feature, one support feature, one passive trait, and optionally one ritual or prep-based feature.
Score every feature with the rubric. Record base cost, modifiers, discounts, and final cost.
Check domain coverage so the sequence is strong where it should be strong without solving every problem at once.
Run the balance checklist. If it overruns the game, reduce reliability, frequency, scope, or setup ease before cutting the fantasy.
One signature trick, one passive, one minor utility or control effect
Signature feature plus one reliable encounter tool and support effects
Stronger rituals, stronger control, or broader battlefield or social presence
Very strong signature effects plus costly or preparation-heavy authority
Few but defining abilities with clear campaign-level consequences
Utility, control, and narrative authority only feel unmanageable when they are judged as vibes. To make them tractable, score each feature on measurable axes and then adjust for limitations.
Step 1: assign a base function score
Single target, few targets, scene-wide, or broad area
Hard to set up, normal reliability, or near-certain effect
Rare or ritual-only, encounter-limited, reusable, or near at-will
Low synergy, noticeable synergy, or abusable layering
Can be interrupted, limited counterplay, or very hard to stop
Meaningful ritual time, setup turns, or advance planning
Consumes money, rare resources, or painful tradeoffs
Using the feature meaningfully endangers the character
Works only in narrow conditions or against narrow targets
Failure causes serious harm, exposure, or lost control
Competes with a very scarce pathway resource
Final feature cost = base function score + impact modifiers - limitation discounts. Use a believable range instead of false precision if needed.
After scoring the feature, tag which play domains it affects. A pathway can be weak in direct combat but strong in investigation or ritual leverage and still be balanced.
Can it win or swing a fight directly?
Can it reveal hidden information or truths?
Can it reach, escape, hide, or reposition unusually well?
Can it compel, deceive, or shape behavior?
Can it reshape outcomes with time and resources?
Can it absorb danger or endure backlash?
No meaningful cost for using power

Use this section as a standing instruction block whenever you ask AI to help build pathway content. The AI should not simply brainstorm cool ideas. It should produce a structured draft that obeys the budget and explains its scoring.
Treat the sequence budget as a cap, not a suggestion.
Preserve pathway identity rather than distributing budget evenly across all domains.
Price features by actual impact in play, not by flavor text.
Bound narrative authority by scope, frequency, preparation, and reliability.
If a package exceeds budget, reduce reliability, frequency, scope, or ease of use before cutting the core fantasy.
Output both the total cost and the reasoning used to arrive at it.
Target sequence and whether the AI is building the total package or only the gain package
Target total budget or gain budget from this guide
Which domains should be strongest, secondary, and intentionally weak
Combat-heavy, intrigue-heavy, investigation-heavy, occult-heavy, or mixed
Anything the pathway must include or must avoid
A short statement of what this sequence changes in play
Target budget, projected spend, and any intentionally reserved budget
Each feature with name, description, domain tags, and cost breakdown
How the sequence spends its budget across offense, control, utility, survivability, information, ritual leverage, and narrative authority
Why the sequence is fair relative to its tier and what keeps it from overrunning the game
Adjudication notes, edge cases, or abuse risks
Create a pathway sequence package for a Lord of the Mysteries-inspired TTRPG.
Use the attached pathway creation guide as the authority for budgeting and balancing.
Package type: [TOTAL PACKAGE or GAIN PACKAGE]
2. Draft 3-6 features appropriate for the sequence.
3. For each feature, provide a cost breakdown using base function score, modifiers, and limitation discounts.
4. Tag each feature by play domain.
5. Keep the total at or below budget.
6. Explain how the package stays faithful to pathway identity while remaining balanced.
7. Flag any feature that may need GM adjudication.
Two Sequence 7 gain packages can spend the same budget very differently and still be balanced.
Enhanced melee pressure and a brief burst of physical dominance.
Danger-sense passive that raises survivability but does not reveal broad hidden truths.
Most of the spend goes into direct offense and survivability.
Spirit vision, omen reading, subtle misdirection, and danger intuition.
Much lower direct damage but stronger investigation, setup, and prep value.
Most of the spend goes into information, utility, and light control.
Does the sequence have a sharp identity rather than a pile of unrelated tricks?
Does the total or gain package stay within budget?
If the pathway is abstract, are its high-impact features bounded by frequency, scope, setup, or risk?
Would this sequence still matter if the campaign de-emphasized its best domain?
Does the package create interesting decisions rather than automatic wins?
Could another pathway of the same sequence oppose it meaningfully in at least some situations?
Does the sequence add something qualitatively new instead of only inflating previous numbers?
At high tiers, does the package change scale without erasing all need for preparation, cost, or counterplay?
Use this as your manual drafting sheet or ask AI to fill it out.
